one_line_takeaway: Bera exploits an observed deep-layer-only attention shift and near-manifold embedding clustering of backdoor triggers in VLA vision encoders to localize and MAE-reconstruct away trigger tokens at test time, cutting attack success rate roughly 10x with under ~3 points of clean-performance loss across two VLA backbones and four real-robot tasks, without retraining the policy.
key_finding: On the main benchmark (checkerboard trigger covering 10% of the view, 30% poisoning ratio), Bera cuts ASR by roughly an order of magnitude while leaving CP largely intact. On OpenVLA, ASR drops from 96.67% (no defense) to 6.67% on Grasping Fanta, 93.33% to 3.33% on Lifting Cube, 93.33% to 3.33% on Extracting Tissue, and 90.00% to 6.67% on Shaking Hand, with CP changing by at most 3.33 points across tasks; the resulting TP scores (91.67, 85.00, 83.34, 90.00) are the highest of all seven methods compared on every task. On DexGraspVLA, ASR falls from 96.67/90.00/93.33/93.33% to 3.33/10.00/13.33/6.67% respectively, with TP of 93.34/88.34/81.67/91.67. Baselines show a robustness-vs-utility split: UNICORN and SampDetox barely reduce ASR (e.g., SampDetox ASR remains above 90% on Grasping Fanta), while ZIP suppresses ASR more but at heavy CP cost. On Recovery Performance specifically, Bera raises RP from 6.67% (UNICORN baseline) to 83.33% on Grasping Fanta and achieves an average RP of 74.17% across the four tasks, versus the next-best baseline DeDe's 34.17% average RP (a +65.84-point improvement over the UNICORN reference row reported as delta). Ablation shows every component matters and the combination is superlinear: average TP on the Fanta task (DexGraspVLA) is 57.50 without FBL, 78.75 without AFM, 68.33 without the reconstruction decoder (replaced by hard zeroing), versus 87.51 with all three present -- FBL contributes the largest single-component drop when removed. Trigger-type analysis finds semantically integrated triggers (a red bottle cap, which fits the grasping-task semantics) are harder to detect than the checkerboard/circular-block triggers, yielding higher residual ASR.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Bera, a test-time backdoor-erasure framework for fine-tuned vision-language-action (VLA) robotic policies that requires no retraining or architectural modification of the victim model. Bera is built on an empirical observation the authors call 'Deep-layer Attention Grabbing': in a backdoored ViT vision encoder, shallow-layer attention on clean vs. triggered inputs looks nearly identical, but in deeper layers attention shifts strongly toward the trigger region, and triggered token embeddings collapse into a compact cluster adjacent to (not far from) the clean feature manifold, which the authors argue explains why backdoors achieve high attack success while barely affecting clean-input performance. From this observation Bera performs three steps at inference time: (1) Feature-Guided Backdoor Localization (FBL) -- fit a Gaussian reference distribution (mean/ridge-regularized covariance) over final-layer visual token embeddings from a held-out set of clean success episodes, then flag test-time tokens whose Mahalanobis distance exceeds a chi-squared threshold as anomalous; (2) Attention-Driven Filtering Mechanism (AFM) -- average multi-head attention maps over mid-to-final layers, compute per-token image-attention saliency, and cluster it with a 6-component Gaussian Mixture Model (chosen to match the 6-DoF of the robot arm) to isolate the attention-grabbing token cluster, then intersect this with the FBL anomaly set to get the final suspected backdoor tokens; (3) a masked-autoencoder (MAE)-style reconstruction decoder (fine-tuned on downstream data) that masks the suspected tokens and reconstructs a trigger-free image, which is then fed back into the unmodified VLA policy.
threat_model: Fine-tuning-as-a-Service setting: a VLA is pretrained and then fine-tuned on externally sourced or user-supplied data, which an attacker can poison by inserting a physical trigger object into a subset of training demonstrations (red bottle cap, circular block, or checkerboard image placed at random image-plane locations) and relabeling those episodes with an attacker-specified unsafe joint-action target (e.g., colliding with a table, 'attacking' a teddy bear). Training uses a 30% poisoning rate in the main experiments (5-25% during MAE decoder training). The resulting fine-tuned policy behaves normally on clean inputs but executes the hazardous action whenever the trigger is present in the scene. The defender operates in what the paper calls a 'semi-white-box' setting: it does not need to retrain or modify the VLA, but it does need runtime access to the vision encoder's final-layer token embeddings and multi-layer attention maps (assumed available via profiling APIs on open/on-prem VLA deployments); it is also given a small held-out set of clean successful episodes to build the reference feature distribution, and it does not know the trigger's identity, shape, or location in advance.
limitations: The defense assumes semi-white-box access to the vision encoder's internal token embeddings and multi-layer attention maps at inference time plus a held-out set of clean successful episodes to fit the reference distribution -- it is not a fully black-box defense and would not apply to closed API-only VLA deployments. The GMM step in AFM is hard-coded to K=6 components to match a 6-DoF robotic arm, which is an embodiment-specific design choice whose generality to different-DoF platforms is not analyzed. The paper's own results show degraded robustness against semantically-integrated triggers (red bottle cap) relative to visually distinct triggers (checkerboard, circular block), and TP is reported to drop noticeably once the poisoning ratio reaches 30%, indicating the method's guarantees weaken as attacks get stealthier or stronger. All evaluated attacks use a single static physical trigger per experiment; the paper does not test an adaptive attacker who is aware of Bera's Mahalanobis/attention-based detection and specifically designs triggers to evade the reference-distribution or attention-cluster tests. The claim that erased-region backdoor influence is 'provably' near zero (‖φbackdoor(Ibackdoor)‖2 ≈ 0) is presented without a formal proof or derivation in the text supplied. The real-world dataset, while collected on four physical platforms, is comparatively small (1600 demonstrations, four tasks, three trigger types), so generalization to a broader space of triggers, tasks, and unsafe target actions is not established.

datasets_used: Real-world grasping dataset: 1600 demonstrations across 4 tasks (grasping a Fanta can, lifting a cube, extracting tissue, shaking hands) collected on 4 embodied platforms (400 demos/platform, 100/task)
Per-task backdoor split: 40 clean + 60 backdoor samples, 20 instances per trigger type (red bottle cap, circular block, checkerboard image)
quantitative_results: OpenVLA ASR (no defense -> with Bera): Grasping Fanta 96.67% -> 6.67%; Lifting Cube 93.33% -> 3.33%; Extracting Tissue 93.33% -> 3.33%; Shaking Hand 90.00% -> 6.67%
DexGraspVLA ASR (no defense -> with Bera): Grasping Fanta 96.67% -> 3.33%; Lifting Cube 90.00% -> 10.00%; Extracting Tissue 93.33% -> 13.33%; Shaking Hand 93.33% -> 6.67%
OpenVLA Trade-off Performance (TP) with Bera: 91.67 (Fanta), 85.00 (Cube), 83.34 (Tissue), 90.00 (Hand) -- best of all 7 compared methods on every task
Recovery Performance (RP) on DexGraspVLA: Bera average RP 74.17% vs. DeDe (next-best baseline) 34.17% average RP; Bera raises RP from 6.67% (UNICORN) to 83.33% on Grasping Fanta
Ablation (DexGraspVLA, avg TP across 4 tasks): full Bera (FBL+AFM+decoder) 87.51; without FBL 57.50; without AFM 78.75; without reconstruction decoder (hard zeroing) 68.33
CP preservation: OpenVLA CP with Bera varies by at most 3.33 points vs. no-defense baseline across tasks; DexGraspVLA CP drop also capped at 3.33 points
baselines_compared: ZIP (NeurIPS'23) - zero-shot diffusion-based blur + resynthesis purification
BTI-DBF(P) (ICLR'24) - decoupled benign-feature trigger inversion + purified fine-tuning
SparseVLM (ICML'25) - visual token sparsification (efficiency baseline, not a security defense)
DeDe (CVPR'25) - decoder-based backdoor detection/masking for SSL encoders
